Award-winning Japanese wines typically come from larger producers like Château Mercian and Katsunuma Jyozo, not small family wineries.

https://vinepair.com/articles/champagne-flavor-map-infographic/
The Ultimate Champagne Flavor Map [Infographic]

Wineries of all types strive to develop an estate identity through their liquid, but nowhere is that brand recognition as important as it is in Champagne. The flagship product for most Champagne maisons is the “house style,” a proprietary blend of grapes from assorted plots and vintages. No two non-vintage recipes are the same.
Champagne must be produced in the Champagne region to bear that label.
Three grapes permitted in Champagne: Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier.
Chardonnay-dominant blends are mineral and light; Pinot Noir-heavy blends are richer and fruit-forward.
Champagne ages in bottle on dead yeast cells (lees) for minimum 12 months; longer aging develops brioche, almond, and creamy flavors.
Each Champagne maison develops a unique 'house style' blend with no two non-vintage recipes identical.

Decanter Fine Wine Encounter London returns this November

Winemakers' Lunches on November 6 limit attendance to six guests per lunch for intimate conversations with prominent winemakers.

French heatwave to cut Champagne yields by 10%

France's prolonged summer heat and drought are shrinking grape development across major wine regions, with Champagne growers expecting yields to fall around 10% and harvests beginning weeks earlier than normal
Champagne yields expected to fall 10% due to French heatwave and drought.
Champagne harvest likely to begin around 15 August, potentially the earliest on record.
Burgundy and Bordeaux producers expect significant output losses but cannot yet quantify precisely.
Prolonged heat increasing sugar accumulation in grapes, potentially affecting alcohol levels and flavor profiles.
Little or no rainfall forecast across France's wine regions before mid-July, extending three-week dry spell.

https://daily.sevenfifty.com/mourvedre-was-made-for-heat-rising-temperatures-are-testing-it/
Mourvèdre Was Made for Heat. Rising Temperatures Are Testing It
In a warming climate, even one of the world's most heat-tolerant grape varieties is reaching its limits
Mourvèdre, traditionally dominant in Bandol, requires heat moderation via altitude or coastal influence to maintain balance as temperatures rise.
Bandol producers use canopy management techniques—gobelet training, higher pruning, shade planting—to protect grapes from extreme sunshine and sunburn.
Drought is forcing reconsideration of dry farming in Provence; some growers now irrigate to prevent vine death, risking terroir typicity concerns.
Jumilla's vineyard surface area halved over 20 years, with only higher-altitude sites (600–800 meters) proving resilient to severe heat and drought.
Bandol's rosé production (75% of region output) may decline as cooler, traditionally rosé-designated sites shift to red wine production due to climate change.

Seven organic Sauvignon Blanc for summer sipping
From fruity and tropical to stony and herbal, there's a Sauvignon style for everyone, if you know where to look. These organic examples are perfect for summer
Sauvignon Blanc is tricky to grow organically because it's sensitive to powdery and downy mildew, requiring regular fungicide treatments in humid years.
Copper-based Bordeaux mixture is one of the only permitted organic fungicides against powdery mildew, but Sauvignon Blanc flowers are sensitive to copper.
Andreas Tscheppe's Blue Dragonfly from south Styria, Austria is fermented with natural yeasts and minimal sulphur, aged nearly two years in large old barrels.
Nearly 20 years of organic farming at Domaine les Eminades in Languedoc has made their Sauvignon more resilient to disease.

So nice to see wines like this coming from the UK. I love what Flint, who are based
Jamie Goode
Flint Vineyard's Pinot Noir Precoce 2025 from East Anglia blends Pinot Noir with early-ripening Frühburgunder and whole bunch fruit.
Wine fermented and aged in concrete tank and neutral oak; 12% alcohol.
Tasting notes: redcurrant, cranberry, and strawberry fruit with fresh finish; scored 92/100.
Available direct from winery at £110 per six bottles, or around £25 per single bottle from retailers.
The Morning Claret
https://themorningclaret.com/p/mountains-and-magliocco-casa-comerci
Mountains and Magliocco
Calabria might be known as the land of wine, but it depends where you go
Simon J. Woolf
Calabria has nine scattered DOCs; only Cirò (eastern region) has achieved significant fame for its Gaglioppo red wines.
Magliocco exists in two variants: Magliocco Dolce (more tannic, eastern Calabria) and Magliocco Canino (retains acidity, extremely late-ripening, possibly related to Sangiovese).
Casa Comerci, a certified organic estate near Nicotera, grows only Magliocco Canino and Greco Bianco in stainless steel tanks with no barrel aging.
Magliocco Canino can ripen as late as early December; Casa Comerci's 2017 rosé was harvested in the first week of December.

https://www.everydaydrinking.com/p/the-sysco-ification-of-wine
The Sysco-ification of Wine
How wine culture's lazy "drink what you like" ethos has failed, and why it's time to break out the pitchforks
Jason Wilson
Gallo controlled 33% of all U.S. wine in 2023 and has since acquired Rombauer, Hahn Family Wines, Massican, Whiny Baby, and Bev canned wine.
The top 11 wine suppliers controlled 71% of U.S. wine as of 2024, with consolidation continuing through mergers and acquisitions.
Independent wine distributors dropped from roughly 3,000 in the 1990s to just over 1,000 today.
Three dominant distributors—Breakthru, RNDC, and Southern Glazer's—controlled 53% of wine and spirits distribution in America as of 2024.
Restaurant wine lists nationwide are dominated by mass-market corporate wines, creating an illusion of choice while limiting consumers' exposure to diverse producers.
